headlines.title: PITTSBURG BANK LOOTED IN DAYLIGHT
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
PITTSBURG, ALE. 17 .- Two youths Tu caneis today entered the Home- ficers with revolvers, took all available bile. cash and escaped in an automo- Cashier John O'Leary with John Turner, bookkeeper, were the only official» in the banking room. Several depositors stood before the teller's windows when the two men entered. Both were heavily armed. While one covered officers and patrons of the bank the scooped together all the cash in night "cooped" fight Jammed it Hurmedly into a band antener drawn, backed out of the door.

headlines.title: I. C. C. ORDER MAKES NULL STATE RATES

headlines.title: NEW JERSEY ORDERS PROSECUTIONS MADE
headlines.fullText:
TRENTON, N. J. Aug. 17-Governor Fielder today directed Attorney General Wescott and the twenty-one prosecutors of the state to prosecute every combination that may exist in New Jersey to boost the price of food.
If & combination is found grand jury action is called for by the governor.

headlines.title: CHICAGO TO PROSECUTE FOOD PRICE BOOSTERS
headlines.fullText: CHICAGO, Aug. 17 .- Inquiry Into the present high price of foodstation begun ners todas better Federal, state and city Sovende held responsible for the recent advance In price was planned.

headlines.title: BUSINESS MEN ASK CHANGE OF TRAFFIC RULES
headlines.subtitle: Forty-Eight Merchants Seek Right to Keep Own Vehicles in Street.
headlines.fullText:
Forty-eight merchants on Houston street south of Ninth street petitioned the city commission Monday to amend wagons from standing in front of their own places of business longer than forty The five petition minutes was signed by praett- eally every merchant on lower Houston street using delivery wagons.
Mayor Milam, when asked if there was a probability of changes in the traffic ordinance snid he believed there was. but he said the ordinance would not be repealed in toto.

headlines.title: FIRST TRAIN IN 3 DAYS FROM MINERAL WELLS
headlines.subtitle: Seven Coaches Bring Flood. Bound Passengers Through.
headlines.fullText:
The first train out of Mineral Wells since last Friday mornt-y arrived in Fart Worth Monday morning at 11 o'clock. It corsisteg of seven coach loads of passengers, most of whom had been trying to get out of Mineral Wells since Saturday.
Outside of inconvenience through delayed mails, Mineral Wells did not suffer any from the flood which put both its railroads out of commission. The Sunday dinners at hotels and boarding houses gave evidence that Mineral Wells' supplies had been cut off, the passengera way. The keepers were unable to get fresh meats and vegetables, it is said.
The train from Mineral Wells Monday morning took four hours and a half between Mineral Wells and Weatherford was responsible for the slow apeed. train schedules on all North Texas roads were restored to normal Monday morning. Some telegraph trouble was still being experienced.

headlines.title: Aviator's Nerve Saves Him From Being Captured
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
PARIS, Au# 17 .- The following story, illustrating the coolness of French aviators, is being recited fr Paris touse
While flying over Alsace an aviator was obliged on account of lack of gaso- While he was filling his tank he was surprised by a strong German patrol.
Unmindful of the presence of the enemy the Frenchman continued to replenish his supply of fuel. The Germans were taken aback and fearing a trap they halted nearby without fir- When his tank was replenished the aviator resumed his theat The Ger mans hoodwinked, then, perceiving fired at the aeroplane they had but without result.

headlines.title: Imported Beer Drinkers First War Sufferers
headlines.fullText:
Drinkers of Imported beer felt the first effect of the European war Monday. They began paying 16 cents a glass instead of 10 cents, and 15 cents a meidel instead of 20.
Sam Joseph sald New York agents of the genuine imported Muenchener beer promise no more than a possible shipment from Germany next week, and they are not sure ef that.
Joseph's has eighteen barrels left dr stock and the usual consumption is one barrel & day.
Imported Muenchener beer is shipped to Hoboken, N. Y. and distributed from Hoboken and St. Louis.

headlines.title: HARTMAN BACK, SAYS CROPS ARE GOOD
headlines.fullText:
8. D. Hartman, assistant county probation officer, returned Sunday night twe Tarrant county boys in the State Training School, Hartman says that crop conditions are excellent all the me tem here to Gatesville and as good madame the school as it onhow. The school expects to raise between 5,000 and 6,000 bushels of corn and has in $50 acres of cotton.
The honor system is working so well with the boy's who have been admitted to special privileges along that fine that plans are being made for the building of another honor cottage.

headlines.title: VICTIMS OF WILD NEGRO ARE CREMATED
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
CHICAGO, ALE. ATThe bodies of the two em mas of Frank Lloyd Wright, who, with their mother and two others, were murdered in Wright's bungalow at Spring Green, Wir, Saturday, will be cremated today.
The house servant, Carleton, & negro employed at the bungalow, who set fire to the place and killed his victims with a hatchet as they rushed from the place, msn aun an modet Wis, where his wife and a servant .m. played by riani The negro is believed to be insane. crime, he shouted, "Self-defense."

headlines.title: SHAMROCK ARRIVES IN GOTHAM PORT
headlines.fullText:
NEW YORK. Aug. 17-Sir Thomas Lipton's Shamrock IV, challenger for the America's cup, ended her bem cent Inntie journey last night, reaching New York harbor shortly before midnight
The Shamrock came in under tow of Sir Thomas Luptana areamn wurde winter, probably after a tuning up. the cup races having been postponed until next year.
The Shamrock left the English coast July 31 and conveyed by the Erin, ar- AuE . Meanwhile the Furocean war had broken out and the Shamrock lay in the harbor at Bermuda while the New York Yacht Club, holders of the America's Cup, were deciding that because of the fur bean confilet the races should be put or and her convoy put out from Bermuda Thursday last for New York.

headlines.title: GERMAN CAVALRY RENEWS OFFENSIVE
headlines.fullText:
PARIS, Aug. 17 .- After remaining virtually inactive for two dasen. troops of German cavalry Daetans un- recently repulsed by the Belgiansan- dertook new offensive operations Balet the Belgian front.
It was evident that the Germ as ama y nesceeded in the di. tection of Wavre, a town fifteen miles southeast of Brussels. During this ad- Southeast or bet amountered the fire of the Belgian outposts and after a number of skirmishes the German of- feprive movement was stopped.
This information was obtained from official sources in Paris today.

headlines.title: COTTON SEED OIL MEN DISCUSS WAR PROBLEM
headlines.subtitle: Mestings Are Being Held at Houston and at Other Points Over the State.
headlines.fullText:
HOUSTON. Aug. 17 .- Fifty cotton seed oil men or conta of South Texas are meeting in Houstonaren to discuss the effect on business.
J. W. Allison, chairman of the bureau of publicity state association, is presiding. A number of meetings are being held over the state auspices of the Texas Cotton Seed Crushers' Association.

headlines.title: FIVE BANDITS KILLED IN CAVE BY DYNAMITE
